title: Noise Resilience of Quantum Key Distribution Protocols Secured Against Independent Attacks With One-Way Communication
abstract: We investigate the resilience to noise of single-qubit quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols in the scenario of security against independent eavesdropping attacks and key distillation based on one-way classical communication. To this end, we introduce a noise-based metric that quantifies the efficiency of QKD protocols. Within this framework, we analyze the maximal noise levels that allow Alice and Bob to asymptotically establish a secure secret key. Using this assumption, we compare the noise tolerance of general single-qubit QKD protocols, in particular the BB84, B92, E91, and six-state protocols. Our main result determines the noise level threshold for QKD allowing one to distill an asymptotically secure secret key. Additionally, we demonstrate that the six-state protocol achieves the greatest resistance to noise while simultaneously yielding a higher post-selection efficiency than the other analyzed single-qubit protocols, confirming its robustness within the considered security model. Finally, we perform an analysis of the proposed noise-based metric and the conventional quantum bit error rate (QBER) metric.

bite.hook: The six-state QKD protocol beats BB84, B92, and E91 in noise resistance and post-selection efficiency.
tldr: The authors evaluated the noise resilience of single-qubit quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols under independent attacks.
They introduced a new noise-based metric to calculate the maximum noise levels that still allow secure key generation.
They proved that the six-state protocol is the most robust against noise and offers higher post-selection efficiency than its competitors.
bite.whyItMatters: Quantum cryptography promises unbreakable security, but real-world quantum channels are noisy, which can disrupt the keys. Knowing which protocol tolerates the most noise helps engineers build more reliable, long-distance quantum communication networks.
bite.eli5: Imagine trying to whisper a secret password to a friend across a windy room. This study compares different 'whispering codes' to find out which one lets you successfully share the password even when the wind is howling.

glossary.shortDef: A secure communication method that uses quantum mechanics to enable two parties to produce a shared random secret key.
glossary.eli5Def: A way of sending secret codes using individual particles of light, where any attempt by a spy to eavesdrop automatically ruins the code and alerts you.

glossary.shortDef: The first quantum key distribution protocol, proposed by Charles Bennett and Gilles Brassard in 1984.
glossary.eli5Def: The original and most famous recipe for sending secure quantum keys using polarized light particles.

glossary.shortDef: The ratio of incorrect qubits received to the total number of qubits transmitted in a quantum channel.
glossary.eli5Def: A percentage score of how many mistakes or glitches occurred while sending quantum information.
